sentences.base_language: I ran for forty minutes in the park.
sentences.target_language: Corrí cuarenta minutos en el parque.
sentences.grammarNote: Correr in the preterite (past tense): corrí (I ran), corriste, corrió, corrimos, corristeis, corrieron. Regular -er verb preterite endings: -í, -iste, -ió, -imos, -isteis, -ieron. The preterite expresses a completed past action.

sentences.base_language: My legs are tired and my muscles are sore.
sentences.target_language: Tengo las piernas cansadas y los músculos doloridos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tengo + las/los + body part + adjective** — a key pattern for physical states.
The adjective agrees in gender and number: **cansadas** (feminine plural, matching **las piernas**) · **doloridos** (masculine plural, matching **los músculos**).
Note definite articles **las/los** instead of possessives — the **tengo** already signals they are the speaker's own body parts.

**estar + gerund** for an action in progress right now (present progressive).
**sudando** is the gerund of **sudar** (regular -ar): drop -ar, add **-ando**.
Spanish present progressive is used more sparingly than English — for truly in-progress actions. The simple present **sudo mucho** can also work for 'I sweat a lot.'

sentences.base_language: I stretch my arms and legs.
sentences.target_language: Estiro los brazos y las piernas.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estiro los brazos y las piernas** — definite articles **los/las** instead of possessives with body parts.
**estirar** (to stretch something/someone) vs. **estirarse** (to stretch oneself — reflexive): **Me estiro** (I stretch myself) · **Estiro los brazos** (I stretch my arms — a specific action on a body part).
Note gender agreement: **los brazos** (masculine) · **las piernas** (feminine).

sentences.target_language: Me duele un poco la rodilla.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me duele un poco la rodilla** — **doler** (gustar pattern): the knee (**la rodilla**) is the grammatical subject.
**un poco** (a little) — adverb modifying the verb, placed between **duele** and the subject.
Note the definite article **la rodilla** — not *mi rodilla*. The **me** pronoun already indicates whose knee it is.

sentences.base_language: I drink water and breathe slowly.
sentences.grammarNote:
**beber** (to drink) — regular -er verb: **bebo, bebes, bebe, bebemos, bebéis, beben**.
Note **agua** (water) without an article here — unspecified water in general, so no article needed.
In other contexts: **el agua** uses masculine article despite being feminine — because it starts with stressed **a-**: **el agua fría** (the cold water).
**despacio** (slowly) — invariable adverb, never changes form.

sentences.base_language: After showering, I feel much better.
sentences.target_language: Después de ducharme, me siento mucho mejor.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Después de + infinitive' = after doing something. When the verb is reflexive (ducharse), the reflexive pronoun attaches to the infinitive and matches the subject: 'después de ducharme' (after I shower), 'después de levantarte' (after you get up).

sentences.base_language: My body feels strong and healthy.
sentences.target_language: Mi cuerpo se siente fuerte y saludable.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Mi cuerpo se siente** — **sentirse** (reflexive) for how something/someone feels: **me siento bien** (I feel well) · **mi cuerpo se siente fuerte** (my body feels strong).
Note **fuerte** and **saludable** are invariable for gender (same form for masculine/feminine).
**saludable** (healthy) comes from **la salud** (health) — related to the greeting **¡Salud!** (Cheers! / Bless you!).
